Module Five Activity¶

For the module five activity, you will write Python code to create clear and informative data visualizations from the provided data set Most-Recent-Cohorts-Field-of-Study.csv. Your code should be well-organized and reflect a clear understanding of each step in the visualization process.

Note: there are two parts to this assignment. Part One is the data visualization coding activity. Part Two is the reflection paper.

Once your code is complete, you will download this notebook as an HTML file by clicking File, then Download As, then HTML (.html) from the Jupyter menu bar. Validate that your code, output, and all six visualizations (rendered as images using the display() function) are visible in the html file before submission.

Missing Blocks: Ensure that you have clicked Run for all blocks before downloading as an HTML file or the block may be missing from your submission. Images must be called using the display() function, for example: display(Image(filename="output/{image_name}.png"))

Specifically, you must address the items from the rubric criteria.

Part One: Data Visualization¶

Prepare the given data set for visualization. Consider the following:¶

  • Are there any columns you can remove?
  • Are filters or groupings needed?
  • Are the data values structured for your chart types?

Clean the given data set for visualization using Python. Consider the following:¶

  • Are there any missing or invalid values?
  • Are there any unclear column names?
  • Are the data types correct for each column (e.g., numeric, categorical, datetime)?

Create multiple types of visualizations using Python. Include the following:¶

  • Dendrogram
  • PCA scatterplot
  • Cluster size distribution
  • Feature importance heatmap
  • Trend chart
  • Scatterplot
Note: You will need to ensure that you have created at least one of each of the visual types listed above.

Part 2: Reflection¶

This part is to be completed in a word document, using the output here to answer the topics.

Identify patterns in the data. Consider the following:¶

  • Are there any clear trends, clusters, or outliers visible in your visualizations?
  • Are certain variables showing consistent behavior or relationships?
  • Are the patterns similar across different visualization types?

Draw conclusions from the patterns. Consider the following:¶

  • Are your conclusions clearly based on the patterns you observed?
  • Are your findings supported by multiple visuals or data points?

Explain how the visualizations validate your findings. Consider the following:¶

  • Are specific chart elements (e.g., color, scale, labels) helping to highlight your findings?
  • Are the insights you found something you could not easily see without the visuals?

Write a summary of the visualization choices you made. Include the following:¶

  • An explanation of the types of charts you used
  • An explanation of the formatting decisions to support visual clarity
To Begin: This Python template program has been broken down into segments as a guide for what steps you should do at a minimum to complete this activity. Look for the # To Do: comments. These are placeholders for you to provide Python code segments. Fill out all # To Do: code segments to complete this program.

Once Complete: Execute a "Kernel restart and run all" to verify that all your code works as a single unit by clicking Kernel, then Restart & Run All before downloading as html.
Hint: If you need to install additional packages, see one of the earlier modules on how to configure and call a requirements.txt file.

Import the Required Packages¶

  • pandas (pd): For reading and manipulating the dataset.
  • MinMaxScaler: A preprocessing tool from sklearn to normalize data values between 0 and 1.
  • PCA: Principal Component Analysis (dimensionality reduction) from sklearn for visualizing clusters in 2D.
  • dendrogram, linkage, fcluster: Tools from scipy for performing hierarchical clustering and visualizing its results.
  • matplotlib.pyplot (plt): For creating charts and saving visuals.
  • seaborn (sns): For advanced and visually appealing charts.
  • numpy (np): For numerical operations like arrays and aggregations.
In [1]:
import pandas as pd   # for reading & manipulating the dataset
from sklearn.preprocessing import MinMaxScaler    # a preprocessing tool from sklearn to normalize data values
                                                                                    # between 0 and 1
from sklearn.decomposition import PCA  # Principal Component Analysis (dimensionality reduction) from 
                                                                   # sklearn for visualizing clusters in 2D
from scipy.cluster.hierarchy import dendrogram, linkage, fcluster    # tools from scipy for performing hierarchical clustering & visualizing its results
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt     #  for creating charts & saving visuals
import seaborn as sns    #  for advanced & visually appealing charts
import numpy as np      # for numerical operations like arrays & aggregations

from IPython.display import Image, display # for image rendering in html output

Part One: Data Visualization¶

Loading the Data¶

Hints: Use pd.read_csv to read a CSV file and load it into a DataFrame (data). Use try block to ensures the program handles errors gracefully if the file cannot be loaded (e.g., missing or incorrect path). Use exit() to stop the program if an error occurs.
In [2]:
print("Loading dataset...")
# To Do: Use the given dataset named "Most-Recent-Cohorts-Field-of-Study.csv" for this assignment
# Load this dataset
# For secure coding, put this action in a try/catch block
Cohorts = None

try:
    Cohorts = pd.read_csv("data/Most-Recent-Cohorts-Field-of-Study.csv")
except Exception as e:
    print("Failed to read CSV", e)
    pd.close()

if Cohorts.notna:
    print("Loaded Cohorts")

Loading dataset...
Loaded Cohorts

Inspect and Select Numeric Columns¶

Hints: Use select_dtypes() to filter columns with numeric data types (float64 and int64). Removes columns like UNITID and OPEID6, which are not useful for clustering. Use dropna() to remove rows with missing values to clean data for clustering, see also module three sample for additional cleaning examples. Use error handling try blocks to ensures the program stops if the column selection fails.
In [6]:
print("Inspecting dataset to select numeric columns for clustering...")
# To Do: In a try/catch block, exclude identifiers 'UNITID' and 'OPEID6', 
#        then print the numeric columns selected for clustering and also print the data for clustering in rows & columns

try:
    numeric_data = Cohorts.select_dtypes(include=['float64', 'int64'])
    
    clustering_data = numeric_data.drop(columns=['UNITID', 'OPEID6'], errors='ignore')
    clustering_data = clustering_data.dropna()
    
    print("Numeric Columns Selected for Clustering:")
    print(clustering_data.columns.tolist())
    
    print(f"Data for clustering ({clustering_data.shape[0]} rows x {clustering_data.shape[1]} columns):")
    print(clustering_data.head())

except Exception as e:
    print("Failed to process numeric columns", e)

Inspecting dataset to select numeric columns for clustering...
Numeric Columns Selected for Clustering:
['MAIN', 'CIPCODE', 'CREDLEV', 'IPEDSCOUNT1', 'IPEDSCOUNT2', 'DISTANCE']
Data for clustering (184861 rows x 6 columns):
   MAIN  CIPCODE  CREDLEV  IPEDSCOUNT1  IPEDSCOUNT2  DISTANCE
2     1      109        3          3.0          9.0         1
3     1      110        3          7.0         10.0         1
4     1      110        5          4.0          6.0         1
5     1      110        6          2.0          4.0         1
7     1      199        3          6.0          6.0         1

Normalize the Data¶

Hints: Use MinMaxScaler() to scale data values between 0 and 1, ensuring all features are on the same scale. Use fit_transform() to fit the scaler to the data and transform it.
In [7]:
print("Normalizing the data using MinMaxScaler...")
# To Do: in a try/catch block, normalize the data using MinMaxScaler

try:
    scaler = MinMaxScaler()
    # Fit and transform the numeric data
    normalized_data = scaler.fit_transform(clustering_data)
    
    # Convert back to DataFrame to maintain column names and structure
    normalized_df = pd.DataFrame(normalized_data, columns=clustering_data.columns)
    
    print("Normalization complete.")
    display(normalized_df.head())

except Exception as e:
    print("Failed to normalize data", e)

Normalizing the data using MinMaxScaler...
Normalization complete.
MAIN CIPCODE CREDLEV IPEDSCOUNT1 IPEDSCOUNT2 DISTANCE
0 1.0 0.001698 0.285714 0.000330 0.000937 0.0
1 1.0 0.001886 0.285714 0.000771 0.001041 0.0
2 1.0 0.001886 0.571429 0.000440 0.000625 0.0
3 1.0 0.001886 0.714286 0.000220 0.000416 0.0
4 1.0 0.018676 0.285714 0.000661 0.000625 0.0

Perform Hierarchical Clustering¶

Hints: Use linkage() to compute hierarchical clusters using Ward's method, which minimizes variance between clusters. Limit clustering to the first 1000 rows for performance. Use fcluster() to assign cluster labels to data points based on the number of clusters (max_clusters).
In [8]:
CLUSTER_SIZE = 1000
In [9]:
print("Performing hierarchical clustering using Ward's method...")
# To Do: in a try/catch block use the Ward's method to perform hierarchical clustering, limit max clusters to 4.

try:
    z = linkage(normalized_df.head(CLUSTER_SIZE), method='ward')
    
    max_clusters = 4
    cluster_labels = fcluster(z, max_clusters, criterion='maxclust')
    
    print("Hierarchical clustering complete.")
    print(f"Assigned labels for {len(cluster_labels)} rows.")

except Exception as e:
    print("Failed to perform hierarchical clustering", e)

Performing hierarchical clustering using Ward's method...
Hierarchical clustering complete.
Assigned labels for 1000 rows.

Generate, Save, and Output Visualizations¶

Each visualization is created, saved and displayed, with text output provided before and after.

Dendrogram¶

Hints: A dendrogram displays the hierarchical structure of clusters. Save the image as dendrogram.png. Use display() to render the image for html file output. Do not use an html image tag in the markdown, or the image will not print in the html download.
In [10]:
import os

if not os.path.exists('output'):
    os.makedirs('output')
In [11]:
print("Creating dendrogram to visualize hierarchical clustering...")
# To Do: Create a dendrogram to visualize hierarchical clustering, and save the dendrogram into a png file under the 'output' folder.
    
try:
    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 7))
    plt.title("Hierarchical Clustering Dendrogram (Ward's Method)")
    plt.xlabel(f"Sample Index (First {CLUSTER_SIZE} rows)")
    plt.ylabel("Distance")
    
    dendrogram(z, leaf_rotation=90., leaf_font_size=8.)
    
    plt.savefig('output/dendrogram.png')
    plt.close()
    print("Dendrogram saved successfully.")
except Exception as e:
    print("Failed to create or save dendrogram", e)

Creating dendrogram to visualize hierarchical clustering...
Dendrogram saved successfully.
In [12]:
print("Dendrogram visualization display...")
# To Do: output the image using the display() function, for example: display(Image(filename="output/scatter_plot.png"))

try:
    display(Image(filename="output/dendrogram.png"))
except Exception as e:
    print("Failed to display dendrogram image", e)

Dendrogram visualization display...

PCA Scatter Plot¶

Hints: A PCA scatter plot projects high-dimensional data into 2D using PCA for visualization. Save the image as pca_scatter_plot.png. Use display() to render the image for html file output. Do not use an html image tag in the markdown, or the image will not print in the html download.
In [13]:
print("Creating scatter plot (PCA Reduced) to visualize clusters in 2D...")
# To Do: Create a PCA Scatter Plot to visualize clusters in 2D, and save the PCA Scatter Plot into a png file under the 'output' folder.

try:
    pca = PCA(n_components=2)
    pca_data = pca.fit_transform(normalized_df.head(CLUSTER_SIZE))
    
    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 7))
    scatter = plt.scatter(
        pca_data[:, 0], 
        pca_data[:, 1], 
        c=cluster_labels, 
        cmap='viridis', 
        marker='o', 
        edgecolor='k', 
        alpha=0.7
    )
    
    plt.title("PCA Scatter Plot of Clusters")
    plt.xlabel("Principal Component 1")
    plt.ylabel("Principal Component 2")
    plt.colorbar(scatter, label='Cluster Label')
    
    plt.savefig('output/pca_scatter_plot.png')
    plt.close()
    print("PCA scatter plot saved successfully.")

except Exception as e:
    print("Failed to create or save PCA scatter plot", e)

Creating scatter plot (PCA Reduced) to visualize clusters in 2D...
PCA scatter plot saved successfully.
In [14]:
print("PCA scatter plot visualization display...")
# To Do: output the image using the display() function, for example: display(Image(filename="output/pca_scatter_plot.png"))

try:
    display(Image(filename="output/pca_scatter_plot.png"))
except Exception as e:
    print("Failed to display PCA scatter plot image", e)

PCA scatter plot visualization display...

Cluster Size Distribution¶

Hints: A cluster size distribution displays the population size of each cluster. Save the image as cluster_size.png. Use display() to render the image for html file output. Do not use an html image tag in the markdown, or the image will not print in the html download.
In [15]:
print("Creating cluster size distribution chart to analyze cluster populations...")
# To Do: Create a cluster size distribution chart, and save it into a png file under the 'output' folder.

try:
    counts = pd.Series(cluster_labels).value_counts().sort_index()
    
    plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
    counts.plot(kind='bar', color='skyblue', edgecolor='black')
    
    plt.title("Cluster Size Distribution")
    plt.xlabel("Cluster Label")
    plt.ylabel("Number of Data Points")
    plt.xticks(rotation=0)
    
    plt.savefig('output/cluster_size.png')
    plt.close()
    print("Cluster size distribution chart saved successfully.")

except Exception as e:
    print("Failed to create or save cluster size distribution", e)

Creating cluster size distribution chart to analyze cluster populations...
Cluster size distribution chart saved successfully.
In [16]:
print("Cluster size distribution visualization display...")
# To Do: output the image using the display() function, for example: display(Image(filename="output/cluster_size.png"))

try:
    display(Image(filename="output/cluster_size.png"))
except Exception as e:
    print("Failed to display cluster size distribution image", e)

Cluster size distribution visualization display...

Feature Importance Heatmap¶

Hints: A feature importance heatmap highlights the average feature values for each cluster. Save the image as heatmap.png. Use display() to render the image for html file output. Do not use an html image tag in the markdown, or the image will not print in the html download.
In [17]:
print("Creating feature importance heatmap to show mean feature values by cluster...")
# To Do: Create a feature importance heatmap, and save it into a png file under the 'output' folder.

try:
    temp_df = normalized_df.head(CLUSTER_SIZE).copy()
    temp_df['Cluster'] = cluster_labels
    
    cluster_means = temp_df.groupby('Cluster').mean()
    
    plt.figure(figsize=(12, 8))
    sns.heatmap(cluster_means, annot=True, cmap='YlGnBu', fmt='.2f')
    
    plt.title("Feature Importance Heatmap (Mean Values per Cluster)")
    plt.xlabel("Features")
    plt.ylabel("Cluster Label")
    
    plt.savefig('output/heatmap.png')
    plt.close()
    print("Feature importance heatmap saved successfully.")

except Exception as e:
    print("Failed to create or save heatmap", e)

Creating feature importance heatmap to show mean feature values by cluster...
Feature importance heatmap saved successfully.
In [18]:
print("Feature important heatmap visualization display...")
# To Do: output the image using the display() function, for example: display(Image(filename="output/heatmap.png"))

try:
    display(Image(filename="output/heatmap.png"))
except Exception as e:
    print("Failed to display heatmap image", e)

Feature important heatmap visualization display...

Trend Chart¶

Hints: A trend chart plots trends in a numeric feature across clusters. Save the image as trend_chart.png. Use display() to render the image for html file output. Do not use an html image tag in the markdown, or the image will not print in the html download.
In [19]:
print("Creating trend chart to analyze a numeric feature across clusters...")
# To Do: Create a trend chart to analyze a numeric feature across clusters, and save it into a png file under the 'output' folder.

try:
    temp_df = normalized_df.head(CLUSTER_SIZE).copy()
    temp_df['Cluster'] = cluster_labels
    
    feature_to_plot = normalized_df.columns[0] 
    trend_data = temp_df.groupby('Cluster')[feature_to_plot].mean()
    
    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 6))
    plt.plot(trend_data.index, trend_data.values, marker='o', linestyle='-', color='tab:red', linewidth=2)
    
    plt.title(f"Trend of Mean {feature_to_plot} Across Clusters")
    plt.xlabel("Cluster Label")
    plt.ylabel(f"Mean {feature_to_plot} (Normalized)")
    plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.7)
    
    plt.savefig('output/trend_chart.png')
    plt.close()
    print(f"Trend chart for {feature_to_plot} saved successfully.")

except Exception as e:
    print("Failed to create or save trend chart", e)

Creating trend chart to analyze a numeric feature across clusters...
Trend chart for MAIN saved successfully.
In [20]:
print("Trend chart visualization display...")
# To Do: output the image using the display() function, for example: display(Image(filename="output/trend_chart.png"))

try:
    display(Image(filename="output/trend_chart.png"))
except Exception as e:
    print("Failed to display trend chart image", e)

Trend chart visualization display...

Scatter Plot¶

Hints: A scatter plot explores the relationship between two features, grouped by clusters. Save the image as scatter_plot.png. Use display() to render the image for html file output. Do not use an html image tag in the markdown, or the image will not print in the html download.
In [21]:
print("Creating scatter plot of two features...")
# To Do: Create a scatter plot of two features, and save it into a png file under the 'output' folder.

try:
    temp_df = normalized_df.head(1000).copy()
    temp_df['Cluster'] = cluster_labels
    
    feature_x = normalized_df.columns[0]
    feature_y = normalized_df.columns[1]
    
    plt.figure(figsize=(10, 7))
    
    # Spread the points out to visualize the density better
    jitter_x = 0.01 * np.random.rand(len(temp_df))
    jitter_y = 0.01 * np.random.rand(len(temp_df))
    
    # Create the scatter plot grouped by Cluster with transparency to show density
    for cluster in sorted(temp_df['Cluster'].unique()):
        cluster_subset = temp_df[temp_df['Cluster'] == cluster]
        idx = cluster_subset.index - temp_df.index[0] # Aligning indices for jitter
        
        plt.scatter(
            cluster_subset[feature_x] + jitter_x[idx], 
            cluster_subset[feature_y] + jitter_y[idx], 
            label=f'Cluster {cluster}', 
            alpha=0.5,
            edgecolors='w',
            linewidth=0.5
        )
    
    plt.title(f"Relationship between {feature_x} and {feature_y} (with Jitter)")
    plt.xlabel(feature_x)
    plt.ylabel(feature_y)
    plt.legend()
    plt.grid(True, linestyle='--', alpha=0.5)
    
    # Save the scatter plot as a png file
    plt.savefig('output/scatter_plot.png')
    plt.close()
    print(f"Scatter plot of {feature_x} and {feature_y} with visual spread saved successfully.")
except Exception as e:
    print("Failed to create or save scatter plot chart", e)

Creating scatter plot of two features...
Scatter plot of MAIN and CIPCODE with visual spread saved successfully.
In [22]:
print("Scatter plot visualization display...")
# To Do: output the image using the display() function, for example: display(Image(filename="output/scatter_plot.png"))

try:
    # Display the saved scatter plot image
    display(Image(filename="output/scatter_plot.png"))
except Exception as e:
    print("Failed to display scatter plot image", e)

Scatter plot visualization display...
